video_title: Why Bitcoiners Are Waking Up to Zcash
gist: Josh Swihart (CEO of Zcash Open Development Lab / Zodel, formerly ECC CEO) lays out the bull case for Zcash as best-in-class privacy money: a recent $25M raise from Paradigm, a16z, Winklevoss, Coinbase and Chapter 1, a product-led 'awakening' driven by the rebranded Zashi/Zodel wallet, Naval's 'Zcash is a hedge against Bitcoin' meme, and migration of disillusioned Bitcoiners. He argues the shielded-pool share (now ~32% of supply, up from ~11% in early 2024) is the key leading KPI and that ZEC is structurally undervalued, while the privacy fight in Washington has a finite political window.

insights.claim: Zcash (ZEC) shares Bitcoin's tokenomics with a 21 million hard cap and is a scarce asset, which Swihart argues makes it terribly undervalued relative to its utility.
insights.speaker: Josh Swihart (CEO, Zcash Open Development Lab / Zodel)
insights.so_what: A privacy asset with Bitcoin-like scarcity but far smaller market cap is the core long-ZEC thesis; watch market cap convergence with Bitcoin as the re-rating signal.
insights.quote: it has the same tokconomics as as Bitcoin 21 mil cap supply

insights.claim: The leverage thesis pitched to investors is that if Bitcoin 10x's from here, Zcash easily 100x's, and the Zodel wallet/company easily 1000x's, framing Zodel as the equity-like high-beta play on the Zcash narrative.
insights.speaker: Josh Swihart
insights.so_what: Sizes the asymmetric upside investors underwrote; the implied ladder (BTC < ZEC < Zodel equity) is the risk/reward menu for exposure to the privacy theme.
insights.quote: if if if Bitcoin is able to 10x from here, Zcash is easily 100x from here. Zotle can easily thousandx from here

insights.claim: Swihart claims Zcash is technically better than Bitcoin and dismisses Bitcoin's greater-decentralization advantage as made up, while conceding Bitcoin has more reach and institutional adoption.
insights.speaker: Josh Swihart
insights.so_what: The explicit 'better than Bitcoin' framing is the meme being sold to migrating Bitcoiners; note he concedes BTC's reach/institutional lead as the gap to close.
insights.quote: I know the Bitcoiners hate this and they gripe or whatever, just technically um it is better. it is better than Bitcoin

insights.claim: Swihart embraces ZEC price volatility as a feature, arguing speculative price exposure is the funnel that converts buyers into privacy users who then self-custody and shield, making the asset reflexive.
insights.speaker: Josh Swihart
insights.so_what: Reflexivity loop (price up -> attention -> shielding -> less liquidity -> price up) implies pullbacks recruit holders; a base of shielded supply underpins the floor.
insights.quote: the more we have some of this price volatility, I think it's actually better for for people to kind of ultimately learn what this asset is

insights.claim: Swihart is agnostic on whether ZEC is store-of-value vs medium-of-exchange, framing it like a dollar that is SoV when saved and MoE when spent, and prioritizing utility (mobility, self-custody, yield, loans) over classification.
insights.speaker: Josh Swihart
insights.so_what: Signals the team will pursue full money/financial-stack utility rather than a narrow digital-gold positioning, widening the addressable use cases.

themes.theme: Shielded pool as the alpha KPI

insights.claim: Shielded-pool share of total ZEC supply rose from about 11% in early 2024 to north of 30% (now ~32%, an all-time high) over two years, versus near-flat in the prior eight years.
insights.speaker: Josh Swihart
insights.so_what: Concrete adoption inflection; rising shielded share is the on-chain metric to track as the leading indicator of genuine (non-speculative) demand.
insights.quote: we went from in the beginning of early 2024 from about 11% of the total supply of Ze at that time uh was shielded to now now north of 30%

insights.claim: Swihart calls the shielded pool the single most important KPI and a leading indicator: when the pool rises, price tends to follow because supply moves off exchanges into illiquid self-custody, reducing tradable float.
insights.speaker: Josh Swihart
insights.so_what: Gives a tradable signal: track shielded-pool growth as a supply-shock proxy; rising pool + flat float is bullish for price.
insights.quote: if you see the shielded pool going up, typically you're going to see the price going up

insights.claim: Swihart offers a divergence framework: price rising while the shielded pool stays flat signals speculation, whereas price rising with a growing pool signals real utility and a durable base.
insights.speaker: Josh Swihart
insights.so_what: A practical filter to distinguish speculative pumps from organic adoption rallies in ZEC; fade rallies where the pool is flat.
insights.quote: when you see the price go up exponentially, a huge price, but the shield size of the shield pool is flat, then it's speculative

insights.claim: The shielded pool is sticky because exiting requires multiple hops (two-to-four) back to an exchange, so high shielded share signals holders prioritizing privacy over liquidity rather than near-term sellers.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Stickiness implies shielded supply is effectively locked float; a high pool reduces sell pressure and supports a structurally higher price base.

insights.claim: Swihart flags a measurement caveat: institutions and whales must custody in transparent (unshielded) addresses for legal compliance, so their inflows can lift price without lifting the shielded-pool metric.
insights.speaker: Josh Swihart
insights.so_what: Tempers naive use of the KPI; institutional bid can decouple price from shielded share, so the pool may understate true demand during institutional phases.

insights.claim: Making it easy to swap in and out of shielded ZEC (e.g., one-click USDC<->ZEC in the wallet) spiked adoption, mirroring how enabling Ethereum staking withdrawals increased staking by assuring an exit.
insights.speaker: Josh Swihart
insights.so_what: Liquidity/exit assurance is the adoption unlock for shielding; UX features that ease entry/exit are the lever to grow the KPI.
insights.quote: we actually made it easier to go in and out with the wallet and the adoption rate spiked

insights.claim: Zashi wallet was spun out of the Electric Coin Company, rebranded to Zodel as a privately owned company, and raised $25M from Paradigm, a16z, Winklevoss, Coinbase Ventures and Chapter 1, with 100% of the former ECC engineering team moving over.
insights.speaker: Josh Swihart
insights.so_what: Marquee crypto VCs co-investing (some who rarely co-invest) is a strong institutional validation of the privacy thesis and the core Zcash dev team.
insights.quote: $25 million you guys raised from, like I said, Paradigm, A16Z, the Winklevi, Coinbase Ventures, uh, Chapter 1

insights.claim: Most investors allocated 75-90% of their Zcash-ecosystem allocation to buying ZEC directly and only 10-25% to funding Zodel equity, signaling the round was primarily a token bet.
insights.speaker: Josh Swihart
insights.so_what: Reveals smart-money positioning is concentrated in the token, not the company; implies sizeable VC ZEC accumulation as a demand tailwind.
insights.quote: they were taking 75 to 90% of that and they were buying or had planned to buy or will buy um Zack and then the remainder 10 to 25% they uh funded um they funded Zotle

insights.claim: The pitch frames Zodel as 'what Coinbase was to Bitcoin' for Zcash, i.e., the onboarding/UX layer that mass-onboards users onto the protocol.
insights.speaker: Josh Swihart
insights.so_what: Positions Zodel as the consumer gateway capturing adoption value on top of Zcash; the Coinbase analogy frames its equity upside.
insights.quote: Zot is to Zcash what Coinbase was to Bitcoin

insights.claim: Launching an 'opinionated' shielded-by-default wallet (Zashi/Zodel) plus added hardware-wallet support and swap support (via Near) drove shielded-pool growth from flat/declining to exponential then vertical, demonstrating product-market fit.
insights.speaker: Josh Swihart
insights.so_what: UX, not protocol, was the binding constraint; the adoption curve inflected on wallet features, so further UX/hardware integrations are the growth catalysts to watch.
insights.quote: We added swap support um in partnership with Nar Intense and it went vertical in terms of in terms of adoption

insights.claim: Swihart governance-reformed Zcash before growth: eliminating the direct dev-funding model and the 2-of-2 multisig governance between the Zcash Foundation and ECC, and open-sourcing additional code.
insights.speaker: Josh Swihart
insights.so_what: Removing governance overhang and dev-funding tax is part of why the asset could re-rate; cleaner governance reduces structural discount.
insights.quote: We got rid of direct dev funding model so that doesn't exist anymore

insights.claim: Building private/shielded software costs roughly 2-3x the engineering of building non-private equivalents, per an unnamed privacy-company founder Swihart cites.
insights.speaker: Josh Swihart
insights.so_what: Quantifies the moat: privacy UX is hard and capital-intensive, raising the barrier for competitors and justifying the team's full-stack advantage.
insights.quote: it probably takes two to threex the engineering to build something uh that's not private than to build something that's private

insights.claim: Swihart rejects the idea that the Zashi->Zodel spinout caused the Zcash awakening, attributing it instead to converging macro forces (AI-driven privacy urgency, Bitcoin financialization backlash) aligning with the product push.
insights.speaker: Josh Swihart
insights.so_what: Frames the narrative as macro-driven and therefore more durable than a single company event; the privacy bid may persist independent of Zodel execution.

insights.claim: Swihart cites Naval's October 2025 tweet framing 'Bitcoin is a hedge against the US dollar and Zcash is a hedge against Bitcoin' as catalyzing inflows and a migration of Bitcoiners hedging into or fully transitioning to ZEC.
insights.speaker: Josh Swihart
insights.so_what: Identifies the specific meme/catalyst driving rotation; 'hedge against Bitcoin' is the narrative hook recruiting BTC capital into ZEC.
insights.quote: Bitcoin is a hedge against the US dollar and Zcash is a hedge against Bitcoin

insights.claim: Bitcoiners are rotating toward Zcash partly out of concern Bitcoin has been captured by TradFi and has drifted from its cypherpunk ethos, treating ZEC as a return-to-roots hedge.
insights.speaker: Josh Swihart
insights.so_what: The disillusionment-with-BTC trade is a structural demand driver for privacy coins; watch sentiment around Bitcoin's institutionalization as a ZEC tailwind.
insights.quote: people were starting to question the financialization of Bitcoin um whether Bitcoin had really been captured um by Tradfi

insights.claim: Quantum risk is an additional migration driver: some holders doubt Bitcoin can become post-quantum before 'Qday' (which Swihart says could hit even before 2030), making Zcash's post-quantum roadmap a differentiator.
insights.speaker: Josh Swihart
insights.so_what: Post-quantum timeline is an emerging catalyst; if Qday fears grow, ZEC's PQ roadmap could attract security-conscious BTC holders.
insights.quote: There's some people that are more concerned about whether or not Bitcoin can get to postquantum in in time for Qday

insights.claim: Zcash is a code fork of Bitcoin plus zero-knowledge cryptography, positioning it as a close relative of Bitcoin rather than an unrelated altcoin.
insights.speaker: Josh Swihart
insights.so_what: The lineage argument lowers the switching narrative for Bitcoiners; 'Bitcoin with privacy' is the framing that eases BTC capital migration.
insights.quote: Zcash was a code fork of Bitcoin plus zero knowledge cryptography

insights.claim: Swihart says a16z's Chris Dixon-circle figure (referenced as Bali/Balaji) called Zcash 'one of the most important projects in the world' (not just in crypto), and personally catalyzed the funding effort after Swihart admitted they were underfunded.
insights.speaker: Josh Swihart
insights.so_what: High-profile endorsement plus active fundraising help signals influential-backer conviction that can drive narrative and capital.
insights.quote: Zcash is one of the most important projects in the world. Not one of the most important crypto projects of the world, one of the most important projects in the world

insights.claim: Swihart says Zcash's 'compliant privacy coin' reputation is NOT primarily because of its transparent/unshielded addresses; regulated exchanges can support shielded deposits/withdrawals in full compliance because they still KYC and can request source-of-funds.
insights.speaker: Josh Swihart
insights.so_what: Reframes the compliance moat as exchange engineering choice, not protocol limitation; undercuts the bear thesis that privacy and compliance are mutually exclusive.
insights.quote: all of those organizations everywhere could be in complete compliance and still support um shielded. They just chose or have chosen not to

insights.claim: Exchanges defaulted to supporting transparent ZEC because in 2016 shielded proofs were too computationally intensive to scale; by the time that was solved (2018) exchanges had already onboarded transparent addresses and saw no commercial demand to do the extra HSM work.
insights.speaker: Josh Swihart
insights.so_what: Explains the path-dependency behind low shielded support; the bottleneck is exchange willingness/HSM upgrades, a fixable adoption lever, not a regulatory ban.
insights.quote: by then everybody had onboarded with Transparenc because it worked just like Bitcoin

insights.claim: Gemini is the standout exchange supporting shielded ZEC withdrawals, doing the HSM/elliptic-curve engineering work because Cameron and Tyler Winklevoss are ideologically aligned with privacy.
insights.speaker: Josh Swihart
insights.so_what: Identifies the on-ramp for actually obtaining shielded ZEC at an exchange; Gemini support is a practical edge and other exchanges adding it would be a catalyst.
insights.quote: buy it on Gemini because Gemini supports sending it to a shielded address

insights.claim: Swihart says Zcash's compliant-vs-Monero-criminal branding is largely memetic/path-dependent rather than a real technical compliance difference, and that 'shielded by default' is a fallacy since Monero's and Zcash's actual privacy both vary by usage.
insights.speaker: Josh Swihart
insights.so_what: Suggests the brand premium ZEC enjoys over Monero is narrative-driven and potentially fragile or transferable; weigh memetics, not just tech, in the privacy-coin trade.
insights.quote: Like a lot a lot of that's mimemetic

insights.claim: Swihart predicts transparent (T) addresses will eventually be deprecated and removed from Zcash as unnecessary, despite disagreement from some in the ecosystem.
insights.speaker: Josh Swihart
insights.so_what: A move to shielded-only would strengthen the privacy thesis but could complicate exchange/institutional support; a contested roadmap item to monitor.
insights.quote: Ultimately I think T addresses get deprecated at some point and go away. They're not necessary

insights.claim: A Winklevoss-linked Zcash treasury company trades on a major US exchange (NASDAQ or NYSE) and Grayscale has filed to convert its Zcash trust into an ETF, supporting a Bitcoin-style institutionalization bull case.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: An ETF filing plus a listed treasury vehicle are concrete institutional on-ramps; ETF approval would be a major demand catalyst to track.
insights.quote: there's the trust from Gayscale which is being filed to be an ETF

insights.claim: Swihart insists the Zcash protocol will not change to accommodate institutional/ETF adoption, distinguishing price-exposure financialization from the protocol's financial-freedom mission.
insights.speaker: Josh Swihart
insights.so_what: Signals protocol stability and no capitulation to institutional pressure; reduces governance-capture risk relative to fears about Bitcoin.
insights.quote: The protocol won't change. We're not going to change the protocol based upon um like this kind of institutional adopt

insights.claim: Swihart reframes cypherpunk values as 'privacy should be normal and accessible to everybody' rather than countercultural rebellion, arguing mainstreaming is true to the roots and not in tension with ETFs.
insights.speaker: Josh Swihart
insights.so_what: This normalization framing is how the team reconciles institutional adoption with ideology, defusing the 'sellout' bear case that dogs Bitcoin.
insights.quote: for me it's that this should be normal

insights.claim: Zcash's roadmap has three priorities: scale to billions of users (high TPS), achieve post-quantum security, and improve usability to one-click flows.
insights.speaker: Josh Swihart
insights.so_what: The three swim-lanes define the catalyst calendar for ZEC; progress on scaling/PQ are the protocol milestones that could re-rate the asset.

insights.claim: Scaling to billions runs through 'Project Tachyon' led by Sean Bowe, which will introduce new, initially counterintuitive UX elements.
insights.speaker: Josh Swihart
insights.so_what: Names the specific scaling initiative to track; its delivery is a key technical catalyst for the scale-to-billions thesis.
insights.quote: there's an effort that's going on with something called Project Takon um that a guy by the name of Sean Bo is is leading

insights.claim: Zodel is being built as a single-currency, multi-chain wallet that swaps in/out of Ethereum, Solana, Bitcoin and stablecoins while keeping the user's ZEC balance and history shielded, aiming to be a bank-like DeFi stack (borrowing, lending, yield) without a bank.
insights.speaker: Josh Swihart
insights.so_what: Expands Zodel's TAM beyond ZEC into a full self-custody financial app; multi-chain swap/DeFi features are the product catalysts driving wallet adoption.
insights.quote: it's access to defy services borrowing lending yield you know the stuff like that that people want um that they would normally expect from a bank account but without the bank

insights.claim: Zodel is a full-stack protocol steward: it maintains zcashd, is helping migrate to the Zcash Foundation's Rust-based 'zebrad' node over the next year, is building a CLI wallet (alpha), and ships light-client services and the flagship wallet.
insights.speaker: Josh Swihart
insights.so_what: Full-stack control (protocol + wallet) is the team's structural moat, letting them push protocol changes like partially-constructed transactions for hardware wallets; concentrated stewardship is also a key-person/centralization risk.

insights.claim: Wallet swap functionality is being expanded from Near to also include Maya for additional routing paths.
insights.speaker: Josh Swihart
insights.so_what: More swap routes deepen wallet liquidity and ease shielded entry/exit, supporting the adoption-via-UX flywheel.

themes.theme: Privacy regulation: window, risk & political fight

insights.claim: Swihart endorses a finite political window (cited via 'Mer' as ~900-1,000 days) to lock in and legalize crypto privacy before the current US administration departs, arguing the priority is to make Zcash too big to kill.
insights.speaker: Josh Swihart
insights.so_what: Frames a time-bound regulatory catalyst/risk; the ~1,000-day clock is the window in which privacy policy outcomes (favorable or hostile) will be set.
insights.quote: we have about a thousand days left before this administration departs

insights.claim: Privacy is conspicuously absent from current crypto legislation (Clarity Act, stablecoin yield, developer protections, securities-vs-commodities), with the main privacy fights being the Roman Storm and Samurai Wallet prosecutions, which have had mixed results.
insights.speaker: Josh Swihart
insights.so_what: Highlights regulatory tail risk for privacy developers/protocols; the lack of legislative cover means enforcement actions remain the binding constraint.
insights.quote: Privacy is just not included in these modern conversations when it comes to crypto and politics in Capitol Hill

insights.claim: Swihart recounts DOJ historically pushing key-escrow/transaction-visibility framed around terrorist financing, money laundering and child porn, and argues such backdoors are dangerous because every government system eventually gets hacked.
insights.speaker: Josh Swihart
insights.so_what: Captures the core regulatory threat model (backdoor mandates) and the rebuttal; relevant to assessing existential policy risk to all privacy coins.
insights.quote: wouldn't it be better if we had keys and we had access to all the transactions and we could keep everybody safe

insights.claim: Swihart names pro-privacy allies inside the establishment: former FinCEN acting director Michael Mosier (took ZK-cryptography briefings) and former DOJ financial-crimes prosecutor Michele Korver, now at a16z.
insights.speaker: Josh Swihart
insights.so_what: Establishment privacy advocates reduce regulatory tail risk and lend policy credibility; their influence is a soft catalyst for favorable privacy outcomes.

insights.claim: An unnamed US agency told Swihart it views ubiquitous on-chain data exposure to foreign adversaries as a massive national security problem yet did not intend for crypto to go mainstream, illustrating that prior administrations recognized privacy's necessity while seeking to kill or control crypto.
insights.speaker: Josh Swihart
insights.so_what: Suggests a latent national-security argument FOR financial privacy that could flip policy; a non-obvious bull catalyst if adopted by regulators.
insights.quote: If all of our citizens data is exposed to foreign adversaries for everybody for for them to see for all time, we just don't intend for crypto to go mainstream

themes.theme: AI as the new driver of privacy demand

insights.claim: Swihart argues AI-powered aggregators that fuse on-chain transaction history, balances and online behavior enable rich queries to profile and manipulate individuals, making default financial privacy newly urgent.
insights.speaker: Josh Swihart
insights.so_what: Positions AI surveillance as the secular demand driver for privacy coins; the AI-data-aggregation threat is the fresh narrative that reignited the privacy bid.
insights.quote: now you have a aggregators that are able to aggregate all this data now aggregating onchain data

insights.claim: Host relays that, per a prior episode with Ari Redboard (TRM Labs), intelligence agencies (FBI, CIA, OFAC) favor society moving on-chain because transparent ledgers make tracing easier, an implicitly bearish signal for transparent-chain privacy.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: If surveillance agencies welcome on-chain finance for traceability, it strengthens the structural case for privacy coins as the necessary counterweight.
insights.quote: these uh intelligence agencies are actually kind of into the idea of society moving on chain because it makes their job easier
